rationale: Ten consecutive Even More Audio Dramas (HH-0281..HH-0290, 2016–2019), all confirmed audio_drama. Three are a contiguous Endryd Haar / Blackshields sub-arc (False War → Red Fief → Broken Chain), the rest are single-shot character pieces spread across loyalist, traitor and neutral perspectives in the late Heresy. All ten have Goodreads ratings.

overrides.synopsis: Fleeing the ruin of Calth, the immortal farmer Oll Persson has guided his small band of refugees—including the psyker Katt—through the warped backways of time and space. Now they are stranded in Andrioch, a half-shattered colony perched on the edge of a chasm that pierces the planet to its core, and the path forward has closed. When John Grammaticus steps from the gloom claiming to know the way home, his counsel that Oll abandon his companions to save himself rings false. Pressed by the figure's certainty and Katt's sudden arrival, Oll draws the athame the Cabal once gave him, and discovers the smiling thing wearing Grammaticus's face is not Grammaticus at all but an Alpha Legionary patiently closing the trap.

With Fulgrim ascended and absent, Lord Commander Primus Eidolon holds the Emperor's Children together by force of will and rite. Among his lieutenants, the principled tactician Archorian believes the Legion still has glory to reclaim—and that Eidolon's appetites are dragging them away from it. When the dispute boils over and Archorian moves to set discipline against indulgence, Eidolon answers by unleashing the Kakophoni, the sonic-warriors warped in his own image. What follows is less a duel of officers than a verdict on what the Emperor's Children have already chosen to become, delivered in a wall of screaming sound that leaves no question who now defines the Legion's soul.

flags.reason: available source coverage does not name a specific geographic setting for the dispute

overrides.synopsis: Vice-Caesari Marcus Valerius of the Therion Cohort has spent the war marching where the Lord of Ravens points him. Now Corax has pointed him at Beta-Garmon, into the largest slaughter the Imperium has yet endured, and every soldier in his column understands that this is the end of their road. Valerius is no longer just a commander, though—he is a man visited by holy visions, who believes the God-Emperor whispers to him on the eve of every charge. Whether those visions are divine grace, the strain of a faith that has nowhere else to go, or something else entirely, they may yet guide the cohort to one last meaningful blow against the Warmaster before the planet swallows them all.

The Imperium is at war for its life, and on Terra the Council that must coordinate that defense is choking on its own procedure. Fabricator General Zagreus Kane's tech-priests are dismissed, talked past, and waved aside by the High Lords—a slight made sharper by the awkward fact that Kane's traitor predecessor Kelbor-Hal still sits on Mars, leaving the Mechanicum's loyalty technically contested. Ambassador Vethorel arrives in the corridors of the Imperial Palace with the unenviable task of negotiating a position of strength for the priesthood of the Machine God while her own delegation fractures around her. The fate of the war effort may turn less on a battlefield than on whether two halves of the Imperium can still hear one another speak.

A defiant planetary lord has refused the Warmaster's summons. Argonis of the Sons of Horus arrives as emissary and, in the candle-lit hall of a man who still believes he can negotiate, calmly recounts what was done to the system of Accazzar-Beta in a single day when its rulers said the same word: no. The tale is precise, almost gentle in its telling, and it leaves no room for any answer except surrender. This is how Horus is binding so many worlds to his cause without burning them: he is teaching the galaxy that the cost of refusal has already been paid by someone else, and showing the receipt.

The forge-world of Xana-Tisiphone has chosen the Warmaster, and Rogal Dorn's answer is annihilation. As loyalist and traitor fleets grind one another to wreckage above the planet, a third force descends quietly beneath the cover of their battle. Endryd Haar, the former World Eater turned warlord of the Cicatrix Tyrannis, has brought his Blackshields—legionaries who swear no oath to Emperor or Warmaster—down through the firestorm to claim something specific from Xana's vaults. They are not here to win Dorn's war, nor to lose it for him; they are here to walk out richer, harder to kill, and more themselves than when they arrived. The greater conflict is, for them, only weather.

The raid on Xana left Endryd Haar's Blackshields battered, short of warriors, and no closer to deciding what kind of fighting force they intend to be. A distress call from an old comrade pulls them toward Duat, a tithe-world held in fief by the World Eaters, and Haar tells his pack the prize is plunder and perhaps a recruit. What he finds on Duat is something else: a discovery that turns the raid from a supply run into a hinge. Meanwhile, far away, Malcador the Sigillite is making delicate overtures, hoping to fold Haar and his stolen weapons into the loyalist defense of Terra. To take the Sigillite's offer is to become an instrument again; to refuse is to face the closing war alone.

At Calth, the Ultramarines and the Word Bearers are mustering side by side, a great reconciliation to wash away the public humiliation of the Word Bearers on Monarchia. Captain Alcaeus, however, is not at the muster. He is still in the ruined Ghaslakh expanse, hunting a greenskin overlord whose head he means to lay at his Chapter Master's feet before he reports for duty. The hunt is righteous, the cause clean; Alcaeus is sure of both. So sure, in fact, that he is failing to see the quieter wrongness gathering inside his own Legion's order of battle—the slow, patient signs that the brothers waiting for him at Calth may not be the ones who will greet him there.

The official war for Ultramar is over, but Sergeant Aenoid Thiel does not believe the enemy has actually left. With a small band of fellow veterans calling themselves the Red-Marked, he is waging a quiet, ugly campaign in the empire's back corridors, hunting the cells and saboteurs that the great battles never finished. A thread from a previous hunt drags them to the moon of Bael and an undersea rigging-station that has been hastily abandoned—stations like this do not abandon themselves. What waits in the flooded levels is the first hard outline of a conspiracy his hunters have started calling Nightfane, and the realisation that someone has been preparing the next blow against Ultramar all along.

Endryd Haar has been betrayed by his own second, Erud Vahn, and woken to find himself chained in the brig of a Sons of Horus warship that has been hunting his Blackshields for months. Vahn, separately interrogated and separately pragmatic, is already brokering deals with the traitor packmasters—deals that might buy the warband its life at the price of becoming something it cannot afterwards stop being. Haar has only the oath he has been carrying since the war began: to find Horus and to kill him. To keep that oath he must first break out of a cell aboard a ship designed to keep men like him from ever leaving it, and outrun a hunting force that has finally, definitively cornered him.

flags.reason: story is shipboard (Sons of Horus warship Cicatrix Tyrannis); no concrete geographic setting in source coverage
